generation.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

Pseudo:

% Define K colors
k_color(3).
k_color(5).
k_color(6).

% Determine if a cell has a K color
has_k(R,C) :- input(R,C,Col), k_color(Col).

% Determine rows and columns containing any K cell
row_has_k(R) :- #count { C : has_k(R,C) } > 0.
col_has_k(C) :- #count { R : has_k(R,C) } > 0.

% Compute min and max rows/cols containing K
min_row(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : row_has_k(R) }.
max_row(MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : row_has_k(R) }.
min_col(MinC) :- MinC = #min { C : col_has_k(C) }.
max_col(MaxC) :- MaxC = #max { C : col_has_k(C) }.

% Compute spans
row_span(RowSpan) :- RowSpan = MaxR - MinR.
col_span(ColSpan) :- ColSpan = MaxC - MinC.

% Define output domain cells
output_cell(R,C) :- R = MinR..MinR+RowSpan, C = MinC..MinC+ColSpan.

% Direct mapping: keep K colors
output(R,C,Col) :- output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Col), k_color(Col).

% Fill background: cells without K color become 0
output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_k(R,C).

% Ensure exactly one distinct non-zero color used
used_color(Col) :- output(R,C,Col), Col != 0.
% At least one non-zero cell
:- #count { Col : used_color(Col) } > 0.
% At most one distinct non-zero color
:- #count { Col : used_color(Col) } = 1.

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

Pseudo:

% Define K colors
k_color(3).
k_color(5).
k_color(6).

% Determine if a cell has a K color
has_k(R,C) :- input(R,C,Col), k_color(Col).

% Determine rows and columns containing any K cell
row_has_k(R) :- #count { C : has_k(R,C) } > 0.
col_has_k(C) :- #count { R : has_k(R,C) } > 0.

% Compute min and max rows/cols containing K
min_row(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : row_has_k(R) }.
max_row(MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : row_has_k(R) }.
min_col(MinC) :- MinC = #min { C : col_has_k(C) }.
max_col(MaxC) :- MaxC = #max { C : col_has_k(C) }.

% Compute spans
row_span(RowSpan) :- RowSpan = MaxR - MinR.
col_span(ColSpan) :- ColSpan = MaxC - MinC.

% Define output domain cells
output_cell(R,C) :- R = MinR..MinR+RowSpan, C = MinC..MinC+ColSpan.

% Direct mapping: keep K colors
output(R,C,Col) :- output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Col), k_color(Col).

% Fill background: cells without K color become 0
output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_k(R,C).

% Ensure exactly one distinct non-zero color used
used_color(Col) :- output(R,C,Col), Col != 0.
% At least one non-zero cell
:- #count { Col : used_color(Col) } > 0.
% At most one distinct non-zero color
:- #count { Col : used_color(Col) } = 1.

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule discovered:
%   The puzzle extracts the smallest axis‑aligned rectangle that
%   contains any cell whose color belongs to the set {3,5,6}
%   (the “magic” colors). Inside that rectangle the cells that
%   originally have a magic color keep that exact color in the
%   output; all other cells become 0.  Hence each example shows a
%   5×5 rectangle where the only magic colour present (3, 6 or 5)
%   is kept and everything else is 0.
%
% Why this works:
%   • In every training example the output is a square of size 5,
%     surrounded by a one‑cell border of 0.
%   • The non‑zero cells in the output are exactly the cells of the
%     input that lie inside the bounding box of all “magic” coloured
%     cells and have a colour from the set {3,5,6}.
%   • Cells with other colours are forced to 0.
%   • The rectangle (MinR..MaxR, MinC..MaxC) is uniquely defined by
%     the extreme rows/columns that contain at least one magic cell,
%     therefore the ASP program yields exactly one answer set.
%
% The encoding uses Strategy A (direct derivation) and avoids all
% prohibited patterns. --------------------------------------------------------------

% --- magic colours -------------------------------------------------
k_color(3).      % keep colour 3
k_color(5).      % keep colour 5
k_color(6).      % keep colour 6

% --- detect cells that have a magic colour -------------------------
has_k(R,C) :- input(R,C,Col), k_color(Col).

% --- which rows / columns contain at least one magic cell ----------
row_has_k(R) :- has_k(R,C).        % R appears in some magic cell
col_has_k(C) :- has_k(R,C).        % C appears in some magic cell

% --- compute the bounding box of the magic region -----------------
%   MinR = #min { R : row_has_k(R) }   (smallest row containing magic)
%   MaxR = #max { R : row_has_k(R) }   (largest  row containing magic)
%   MinC = #min { C : col_has_k(C) }   (smallest column containing magic)
%   MaxC = #max { C : col_has_k(C) }   (largest  column containing magic)

min_row(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : row_has_k(R) }.
max_row(MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : row_has_k(R) }.
min_col(MinC) :- MinC = #min { C : col_has_k(C) }.
max_col(MaxC) :- MaxC = #max { C : col_has_k(C) }.

% --- cells that belong to the output rectangle --------------------
output_cell(R,C) :- R = MinR..MaxR, C = MinC..MaxC.

% --- direct derivation: keep magic colour, fill background with 0 --
output(R,C,Col) :- output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Col), k_color(Col).

output(R,C,0)   :- output_cell(R,C), not has_k(R,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The rules are deterministic:
%   * every cell inside the rectangle gets exactly one colour
%     (its original magic colour or 0),
%   * no other colour can appear,
%   * the rectangle is uniquely defined by the extreme magic cells,
%   therefore the solver returns exactly one answer set per example.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule discovered:
%   The puzzle extracts the smallest axis‑aligned rectangle that
%   contains any cell whose colour belongs to the set {3,5,6}
%   (the “magic” colours). Inside that rectangle the cells that
%   originally have a magic colour keep that exact colour in the
%   output; all other cells become 0.  Hence each example shows a
%   5×5 rectangle where the only magic colour present (3, 6 or 5)
%   is kept and everything else is 0.
%
% Why this works:
%   • In every training example the output is a square of size 5,
%     surrounded by a one‑cell border of 0.
%   • The non‑zero cells in the output are exactly the cells of the
%     input that lie inside the bounding box of all “magic” coloured
%     cells and have a colour from the set {3,5,6}.
%   • Cells with other colours are forced to 0.
%   • The rectangle (MinR..MaxR, MinC..MaxC) is uniquely defined by
%     the extreme rows/columns that contain at least one magic cell,
%     therefore the ASP program yields exactly one answer set.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% --- magic colours -------------------------------------------------
k_color(3).      % keep colour 3
k_color(5).      % keep colour 5
k_color(6).      % keep colour 6

% --- detect cells that have a magic colour -------------------------
has_k(R,C) :- input(R,C,Col), k_color(Col).

% --- which rows / columns contain at least one magic cell ----------
row_has_k(R) :- has_k(R,C).        % R appears in some magic cell
col_has_k(C) :- has_k(R,C).        % C appears in some magic cell

% --- compute the bounding box of the magic region -----------------
min_row(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : row_has_k(R) }.
max_row(MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : row_has_k(R) }.
min_col(MinC) :- MinC = #min { C : col_has_k(C) }.
max_col(MaxC) :- MaxC = #max { C : col_has_k(C) }.

% Helper predicate that positively binds the four extreme values
box(MinR,MaxR,MinC,MaxC) :- min_row(MinR), max_row(MaxR), min_col(MinC), max_col(MaxC).

% --- cells that belong to the output rectangle --------------------
output_cell(R,C) :- box(MinR,MaxR,MinC,MaxC),
                  R = MinR..MaxR,

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

%   The puzzle extracts the smallest axis‑aligned rectangle that

%   originally have a magic color keep that exact color in the

%     surrounded by a one‑cell border of 0.
%   • The non‑zero cells in the output are exactly the cells of the
%     input that lie inside the bounding box of all “magic” coloured
%     cells and have a colour from the set {3,5,6}.
%   • Cells with other colours are forced to 0.

%     therefore the ASP program yields exactly one answer set.

k_color(5).      % keep colour 5
k_color(6).      % keep colour 6

output(R,C,Col) :- output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Col), k_color(Col).

output(R,C,0)   :- output_cell(R,C), not has_k(R,C).

% Magic colours
k_color(3). k_color(5). k_color(6).

% Detect cells with magic colour
has_k(R,C) :- input(R,C,Col), k_color(Col).

% Row/column contains magic cell
row_has_k(R) :- has_k(R,C). % positive literal
col_has_k(C) :- has_k(R,C).

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule discovered:
%   The puzzle extracts the smallest axis‑aligned rectangle that
%   contains any cell whose colour belongs to the set {3,5,6}
%   (the “magic” colours). Inside that rectangle the cells that
%   originally have a magic colour keep that exact colour in the
%   output; all other cells become 0.  Hence each example shows a
%   5×5 rectangle where the only magic colour present (3, 6 or 5)
%   is kept and everything else is 0.
%
% Why this works:
%   • In every training example the output is a square of size 5,
%     surrounded by a one‑cell border of 0.
%   • The non‑zero cells in the output are exactly the cells of the
%     input that lie inside the bounding box of all “magic” coloured
%     cells and have a colour from the set {3,5,6}.
%   • Cells with other colours are forced to 0.
%   • The rectangle (MinR..MaxR, MinC..MaxC) is uniquely defined by
%     the extreme rows/columns that contain at least one magic cell,
%     therefore the ASP program yields exactly one answer set.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% --- magic colours -------------------------------------------------
k_color(3).      % keep colour 3
k_color(5).      % keep colour 5
k_color(6).      % keep colour 6

% --- detect cells that have a magic colour -------------------------
has_k(R,C) :- input(R,C,Col), k_color(Col).

% --- which rows / columns contain at least one magic cell ----------
row_has_k(R) :- has_k(R,C).        % R appears in some magic cell
col_has_k(C) :- has_k(R,C).        % C appears in some magic cell

% --- compute the bounding box of the magic region -----------------
min_row(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : row_has_k(R) }.
max_row(MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : row_has_k(R) }.
min_col(MinC) :- MinC = #min { C : col_has_k(C) }.
max_col(MaxC) :- MaxC = #max { C : col_has_k(C) }.

% Helper predicate that positively binds the four extreme values
box(MinR,MaxR,MinC,MaxC) :- min_row(MinR), max_row(MaxR), min_col(MinC), max_col(MaxC).

% --- cells that belong to the output rectangle --------------------
output_cell(R,C) :- box(MinR,MaxR,MinC,MaxC),
                  R = MinR..MaxR,
                  C = MinC..MaxC.

% --- direct derivation: keep magic colour, fill background with 0 --
output(R,C,Col) :- output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Col), k_color(Col).
output(R,C,0)   :- output_cell(R,C), not has_k(R,C).

% The two rules above guarantee that every cell inside the rectangle
% receives exactly one colour (its original magic colour or 0) and that
% no other colour can appear.  The rectangle is uniquely defined by the
% extreme magic cells, so the solver returns exactly one answer set per
% example.
```

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

% ---------- Color domain ----------
color(0..9).

% ---------- Helper predicates ----------
% Count of each color (excluding 0)
count(C,Count) :-
    Count = #count { R,C2 : input(R,C2,C), C != 0 }.

% Find the maximum count value among all colors
maxCount(Max) :-
    Max = #max { Count : count(C,Count) }.

% Find the dominant color D: a color with count = maxCount, and any other color has a strictly smaller count
dominant_color(D) :-
    D != 0,
    count(D,Max),
    % ensure uniqueness: for any other color C the count is < Max
    #not (count(C,Count), C != 0, Count > Max, C != D).

% We enforce uniqueness with a constraint later.

% Define even colors (0,2,4,6,8)
even(C) :-
    C = 0; C = 2; C = 4; C = 6; C = 8.

% Determine output color O from dominant D
output_color(O,D) :-
    even(D), D > 0, O = D - 2.
output_color(O,D) :-
    not even(D), O = D.

% Find the bounding box of the dominant color D
% min row and column where D appears
minRow(MR) :- MR = #min { R : input(R,_,D) }.
maxRow(MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : input(R,_,D) }.
minCol(MC) :- MC = #min { C : input(_,C,D) }.
maxCol(MCol) :- MCol = #max { C : input(_,C,D) }.

% Height and width of the output grid are exactly the bounding box dimensions
height(H) :- H = MaxR + 1.
width(W) :- W = MCol + 1.

% Count of D in each column of the bounding box
countInCol(C,CountCol) :-
    CountCol = #count { R : input(R,C,D) }.

% For each column C we compute the first row that must be O (gravity)
startRow(C,Start) :-
    Start = MaxR - CountCol + 1.

% Output grid domain
output_cell(R,C) :-
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% Exactly one output value per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Constraints to enforce gravity rule
% Cells above startRow(C) must be 0
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), R < StartRow(C), V != 0.
% Cells at or below startRow(C) must be O
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), R >= StartRow(C), V != O.

% No other colors may appear
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, V1 != 0, V1 != O.

% Ensure O is defined (O != 0)
:- dominant_color(D), output_color(O,D), O != 0.

% End.

```asp
% Transformation rule:
% 1. Find the most frequent non‑zero color D in the input.
%    D is the color with the maximum count of occurrences.
% 2. If D is odd keep it (output color O = D),
%    otherwise replace it by D‑2 (output color O = D‑2).  (O ≠ 0)
% 3. For each column inside the bounding box of D, the O‑cells occupy the
%    lowest |{cells with colour D in that column}| rows (gravity);
%    all other cells are 0.
% 4. The output grid size is exactly the bounding box of D.
% The program follows this rule using ASP aggregates, auxiliary predicates
% and cardinality constraints.

% ---------- Auxiliary ----------
% Count of each colour (excluding 0)
count(C,Count) :-
    Count = #count { R,C2 : input(R,C2,C), C != 0 }.

% Maximum count among colours
maxCount(Max) :-
    Max = #max { Count : count(C,Count) }.

% Dominant colour D (colour with the maximum count, unique)
dominant_color(D) :-
    D != 0,
    count(D,Max),
    % ensure uniqueness – at most one colour has the maximal count
    #not (count(C,Count), C != 0, Count > Max, C != D).

% Even colours (0,2,4,6,8)
even(C) :-
    C = 0; C = 2; C = 4; C = 6; C = 8.

% Map dominant colour D to the colour used in the output
output_color(O,D) :-
    even(D), D != 0, O = D - 2.
output_color(O,D) :-
    not even(D), O = D.

% Bounding box of D
minRow(MR) :- MR = #min { R : input(R,_,D) }.
maxRow(MR) :- MR = #max { R : input(R,_,D) }.
minCol(MC) :- MC = #min { C : input(_,C,D) }.
maxCol(MC) :- MC = #max { C : input(_,C,D) }.

% Output dimensions are exactly the bounding box of D
height(H) :- H = maxRow(MR) + 1.
width(W)  :- W = maxCol(MC) + 1.

% For each column C of the output grid we compute the first row that
% must contain an O‑cell (gravity).  The row index is
%    start(C,SR) = maxRow - (# of D‑cells in column C) + 1.
start(C,SR) :-
    CountCol = #count { R : input(R,C,D) },
    SR = maxRow(MR) - CountCol + 1.

% Output cell domain (rows 0..H‑1, columns 0..W‑1)
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% Exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Gravity constraints
% Cells above the gravity line must be 0
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), start(C,SR), R < SR, V != 0.
% Cells at or below the gravity line must be the output colour O
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), start(C,SR), R >= SR, V != O.

% No other colours may appear
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, V1 != 0, V1 != O.

% O must be non‑zero
:- dominant_color(D), output_color(O,D), O = 0.

% Ensure a single dominant colour (at most one D)
:- dominant_color(D1), dominant_color(D2), D1 != D2.
```

candidates.response:
**Explanation of the transformation rule**

The examples show a clear pattern:

1. **Most frequent colour** – In each input there is a non‑zero colour that occurs most often.  
   Call this colour **D** (dominant colour).

2. **Output colour** –  
   *If D is odd* → the colour that survives to the output is **O = D**.  
   *If D is even* → the surviving colour is **O = D – 2** (e.g. 8 → 6, 6 → 4, 2 → 0).  
   The colour **0** is never kept, therefore O ≠ 0 for the three given examples.

3. **Gravity** – Inside the *bounding box* of the dominant colour D the cells with colour D “fall” to the bottom of their column (gravity).  
   For each column the number of O‑cells equals the number of D‑cells that appear in that column, and they occupy the lowest rows. All other cells of the bounding box are 0.

4. **Output size** – The output grid is exactly the bounding box of D (its smallest rectangle containing all D‑cells).

The ASP program below implements this rule generically: it discovers D by counting colours, derives O by the odd/even rule, determines the bounding box of D, computes the gravity “start‑row’’ for each column, and finally forces the output cells to be either 0 (above the start‑row) or O (below it).  The constraints eliminate all wrong colourings while the cardinality statement guarantees exactly one colour per output cell.

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% ARC‑AGI puzzle – colour‑gravity rule
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Find the dominant (most frequent) non‑zero colour D.
% 2. Derive the output colour O: keep D if it is odd, otherwise D‑2.
% 3. Bounding box of D = minimal rectangle containing all D‑cells.
%    Output dimensions = that rectangle.
% 4. Gravity: for each column, O‑cells occupy the lowest
%    |{cells with colour D in that column}| rows.
% 5. Cells above the gravity line are 0, cells at/ below are O.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- auxiliary counting ----------
count(C,Count) :-
    Count = #count { R,C2 : input(R,C2,C), C != 0 }.

% maximum count among all colours
maxCount(MaxCount) :-
    MaxCount = #max { Count : count(C,Count) }.

% dominant colour D (unique colour with the maximal count)
dominant_color(D) :-
    D != 0,
    count(D,MaxCount),
    #not (count(C,Count), C != 0, Count > MaxCount, C != D).

% ---------- parity ----------
even(C) :-
    C = 0; C = 2; C = 4; C = 6; C = 8.
even_not(C) :- not even(C).

% map dominant colour D to the colour that really appears in the output
output_color(O,D) :-
    even(D), D != 0, O = D - 2.
output_color(O,D) :-
    even_not(D), O = D.

% ---------- bounding box of D ----------
minRow(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,D) }.
maxRow(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,D) }.
minCol(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min { C : input(_,C,D) }.
maxCol(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,D) }.

% Output dimensions are exactly the bounding box of D
height(H) :- H = Rmax + 1.
width(W)  :- W = Cmax + 1.

% For each column C of the output grid compute the first row that must be O
% (gravity line).  start(C,SR) = maxRow(D) – #D‑cells in column C + 1.
start(C,SR) :-
    CountCol = #count { R : input(R,C,D) },
    SR = Rmax - CountCol + 1.

% ---------- output cell domain ----------
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% Exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ---------- gravity constraints ----------
% cells above the gravity line must be 0
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), start(C,SR), R < SR, V != 0.
% cells at/ below the gravity line must be the output colour O
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), start(C,SR), R >= SR, V != O.

% no other colours may appear
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, V1 != 0, V1 != O.

% O must be non‑zero
:- dominant_color(D), output_color(O,D), O = 0.

% ---------- uniqueness of dominant colour ----------
% at most one dominant colour may be true
:- dominant_color(D1), dominant_color(D2), D1 != D2.
```

The program:

* discovers the dominant colour `D` by counting colours and taking the colour with maximal count,
* determines the surviving colour `O` (odd → keep, even → subtract 2),
* defines the output grid as the exact bounding box of `D`,
* for each column computes the “gravity line’’ `SR` (the first row that must contain an `O`‑cell),
* forces cells **above** `SR` to be `0` and cells **at/ below** `SR` to be `O`,
* guarantees exactly one colour per cell and that no other colour may appear,
* eliminates the illegal situation `O = 0`.

Together these constraints enforce the exact transformation observed in the three training examples while remaining generic for any ARC‑AGI puzzle that follows this rule.

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% auxiliary counting
count(C,Count) :-
    Count = #count { R,C2 : input(R,C2,C), C != 0 }.

% max count
maxCount(MaxCount) :-
    MaxCount = #max { Count : count(C,Count) }.

% dominant colour D (non‑zero, has maximal count)
dominant_color(D) :-
    D != 0,
    count(D,MaxCount).

% output colour O based on parity of D
output_color(O,D) :-
    D != 0,
    D \ 2 = 0,
    O = D - 2.
output_color(O,D) :-
    D != 0,
    D \ 2 != 0,
    O = D.

% bounding box of D
minRow(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,D) }.
maxRow(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,D) }.
minCol(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min { C : input(_,C,D) }.
maxCol(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,D) }.

% output dimensions
height(H) :- H = Rmax + 1.
width(W)  :- W = Cmax + 1.

% gravity start row for column C
start(C,SR) :-
    CountCol = #count { R : input(R,C,D) },
    SR = Rmax - CountCol + 1.

% output cell domain
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% gravity constraints
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), start(C,SR), R < SR, V != 0.
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), start(C,SR), R >= SR, V != O.

% no other colours may appear in a cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, V1 != 0, V1 != O.

% O must be non‑zero
:- dominant_color(D), output_color(O,D), O != 0.

% at most one dominant colour
:- dominant_color(D1), dominant_color(D2), D1 != D2.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% ARC‑AGI puzzle – colour‑gravity rule (fixed syntax)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Find the dominant (most frequent) non‑zero colour D.
% 2. Derive the output colour O: keep D if it is odd, otherwise D‑2.
% 3. Bounding box of D = minimal rectangle containing all D‑cells.
%    Output dimensions = that rectangle.
% 4. Gravity: for each column, O‑cells occupy the lowest
%    |{cells with colour D in that column}| rows.
% 5. Cells above the gravity line are 0, cells at/ below are O.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- auxiliary counting ----------
count(C,Count) :-
    Count = #count { R,C2 : input(R,C2,C), C != 0 }.

% maximum count among all colours
maxCount(MaxCount) :-
    MaxCount = #max { Count : count(C,Count) }.

% dominant colour D (non‑zero, has maximal count)
dominant_color(D) :-
    D != 0,
    count(D,MaxCount).

% output colour O based on parity of D
output_color(O,D) :-
    D != 0,
    D \ 2 = 0,
    O = D - 2.
output_color(O,D) :-
    D != 0,
    D \ 2 != 0,
    O = D.

% bounding box of D
minRow(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,D) }.
maxRow(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,D) }.
minCol(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min { C : input(_,C,D) }.
maxCol(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,D) }.

% output dimensions are exactly the bounding box of D
height(H) :- H = Rmax + 1.
width(W)  :- W = Cmax + 1.

% For each column C compute the first row that must be O (gravity line)
start(C,SR) :-
    CountCol = #count { R : input(R,C,D) },
    SR = Rmax - CountCol + 1.

% Output cell domain
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% Exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ---------- gravity constraints ----------
% cells above the gravity line must be 0
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), start(C,SR), R < SR, V != 0.
% cells at/ below the gravity line must be the output colour O
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), start(C,SR), R >= SR, V != O.
% no other colours may appear in a cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, V1 != 0, V1 != O.

% O must be non‑zero
:- dominant_color(D), output_color(O,D), O != 0.

% at most one dominant colour
:- dominant_color(D1), dominant_color(D2), D1 != D2.
```

% ARC‑AGI puzzle – colour‑gravity rule
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Find the dominant (most frequent) non‑zero colour D.
% 2. Derive the output colour O: keep D if it is odd, otherwise D‑2.
% 3. Bounding box of D = minimal rectangle containing all D‑cells.
%    Output dimensions = that rectangle.
% 4. Gravity: for each column, O‑cells occupy the lowest
%    |{cells with colour D in that column}| rows.
% 5. Cells above the gravity line are 0, cells at/ below are O.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- auxiliary counting ----------
count(C,Count) :-
    Count = #count { R,C2 : input(R,C2,C), C != 0 }.

% maximum count among all colours
maxCount(MaxCount) :-
    MaxCount = #max { Count : count(C,Count) }.

% dominant colour D (unique colour with the maximal count)
dominant_color(D) :-
    D != 0,
    count(D,MaxCount),
    #not (count(C,Count), C != 0, Count > MaxCount, C != D).

% ---------- parity ----------
even(C) :-
    C = 0; C = 2; C = 4; C = 6; C = 8.
even_not(C) :- not even(C).

% map dominant colour D to the colour that really appears in the output
output_color(O,D) :-
    even(D), D != 0, O = D - 2.
output_color(O,D) :-
    even_not(D), O = D.

% ---------- bounding box of D ----------
minRow(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,D) }.
maxRow(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,D) }.
minCol(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min { C : input(_,C,D) }.
maxCol(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,D) }.

% Output dimensions are exactly the bounding box of D
height(H) :- H = Rmax + 1.
width(W)  :- W = Cmax + 1.

% For each column C of the output grid compute the first row that must be O
% (gravity line).  start(C,SR) = maxRow(D) – #D‑cells in column C + 1.
start(C,SR) :-
    CountCol = #count { R : input(R,C,D) },
    SR = Rmax - CountCol + 1.

% ---------- output cell domain ----------
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% Exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ---------- gravity constraints ----------
% cells above the gravity line must be 0
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), start(C,SR), R < SR, V != 0.
% cells at/ below the gravity line must be the output colour O
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), start(C,SR), R >= SR, V != O.

% no other colours may appear
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, V1 != 0, V1 != O.

% O must be non‑zero
:- dominant_color(D), output_color(O,D), O = 0.

% ---------- uniqueness of dominant colour ----------
% at most one dominant colour may be true
:- dominant_color(D1), dominant_color(D2), D1 != D2.

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.

output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% cells at/ below the gravity line must be the output colour O

% no other colours may appear in a cell

% O must be non‑zero
:- dominant_color(D), output_color(O,D), O != 0.

% at most one dominant colour
:- dominant_color(D1), dominant_color(D2), D1 != D2.

% ---- auxiliary counting ----

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% ARC‑AGI puzzle – colour‑gravity rule (fixed syntax)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Find the dominant (most frequent) non‑zero colour D.
% 2. Derive the output colour O: keep D if it is odd, otherwise D‑2.
% 3. Bounding box of D = minimal rectangle containing all D‑cells.
%    Output dimensions = that rectangle.
% 4. Gravity: for each column, O‑cells occupy the lowest
%    |{cells with colour D in that column}| rows.
% 5. Cells above the gravity line are 0, cells at/ below are O.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- auxiliary counting ----------
count(C,Count) :-
    Count = #count { R,C2 : input(R,C2,C), C != 0 }.

% maximum count among all colours
maxCount(MaxCount) :-
    MaxCount = #max { Count : count(C,Count) }.

% dominant colour D (non‑zero, has maximal count)
dominant_color(D) :-
    D != 0,
    count(D,MaxCount).

% output colour O based on parity of D
output_color(O,D) :-
    D != 0,
    D \ 2 = 0,
    O = D - 2.
output_color(O,D) :-
    D != 0,
    D \ 2 != 0,
    O = D.

% bounding box of D
minRow(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,D) }.
maxRow(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,D) }.
minCol(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min { C : input(_,C,D) }.
maxCol(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,D) }.

% output dimensions are exactly the bounding box of D
height(H) :- H = Rmax + 1.
width(W)  :- W = Cmax + 1.

% For each column C compute the first row that must be O (gravity line)
start(C,SR) :-
    CountCol = #count { R : input(R,C,D) },
    SR = Rmax - CountCol + 1.

% Output cell domain
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% Exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ---------- gravity constraints ----------
% cells above the gravity line must be 0
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), start(C,SR), R < SR, V != 0.
% cells at/ below the gravity line must be the output colour O
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), start(C,SR), R >= SR, V != O.
% no other colours may appear in a cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, V1 != 0, V1 != O.

% O must be non‑zero
:- dominant_color(D), output_color(O,D), O != 0.

% at most one dominant colour
:- dominant_color(D1), dominant_color(D2), D1 != D2.

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% O must be non‑zero
:- dominant_color(D), output_colour(O), O != 0.

% at most one dominant colour
:- dominant_color(D1), dominant_color(D2), D1 != D2.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% ARC‑AGI puzzle – colour‑gravity rule (fixed syntax)
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- auxiliary counting ----------
count_color(C,Count) :-
    C != 0,
    Count = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

maxCount(MaxCount) :-
    MaxCount = #max { Count : count_color(C,Count) }.

dominant_color(D) :-
    D != 0,
    count_color(D,MaxCount).

% ---------- bounding box of the dominant colour ----------
minRow(Rmin) :-
    dominant_color(D),
    Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,D) }.

maxRow(Rmax) :-
    dominant_color(D),
    Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,D) }.

minCol(Cmin) :-
    dominant_color(D),
    Cmin = #min { C : input(_,C,D) }.

maxCol(Cmax) :-
    dominant_color(D),
    Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,D) }.

% ---------- output dimensions ----------
height(H) :-
    H = Rmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    W = Cmax + 1.

% ---------- gravity line for each column ----------
start(C,SR) :-
    dominant_color(D),
    N = #count { R : input(R,C,D) },
    SR = Rmax - N + 1.

% ---------- domain of output cells ----------
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H),
    width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% ---------- exactly one colour per output cell ----------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ---------- output colour derived from the dominant colour ----------
output_colour(O) :-
    dominant_color(D),
    D \ 2 = 0,
    O = D - 2.

output_colour(O) :-
    dominant_color(D),
    D \ 2 != 0,
    O = D.

% ---------- gravity constraints ----------
% cells above the gravity line must be 0
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), start(C,SR), R < SR, V != 0.

% cells at/ below the gravity line must be the output colour O
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), start(C,SR), R >= SR, V != O.

% O must be non‑zero
:- dominant_color(D), output_colour(O), O != 0.

% at most one dominant colour
:- dominant_color(D1), dominant_color(D2), D1 != D2.
```

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

% Determine the most frequent non-zero color

special(S) :- maxColor(S), S = #min { X : maxColor(X) }.

% Compute bounding box of S cells
minr(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,S) }.
maxr(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,S) }.
minc(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min { C : input(_,C,S) }.
maxc(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,S) }.

% Output domain
output_cell(R,C) :- minr(Rmin), maxr(Rmax), minc(Cmin), maxc(Cmax), R = Rmin, Rmax, C = Cmin, Cmax.

% Output values
output(R,C,S) :- output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,S).
output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not output(R,C,S).

% No output outside the rectangle
:- output(R,C,V), not output_cell(R,C), color(V).

% Disallow any other colors within rectangle
:- output(R,C,V), V != S, V != 0, output_cell(R,C).

% Ensure at most one color per cell (uniqueness)
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).

```asp
% -------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   1. Find the most frequent non‑zero color in the input.
%   2. Keep this color (call it S) unchanged.
%   3. The output grid is exactly the Cartesian product of
%      rows and columns that contain at least one S.
%   4. Inside this rectangle cells are S where the input has S,
%      otherwise 0.
%   5. No other colors may appear.
%   6. Exactly one color per cell must be present.
% -------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- 1. frequency of each colour (ignoring colour 0) -----
maxCount = #max { C : color(C), C != 0, #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) } }.

maxColor(C)    :- #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) } = maxCount, C != 0.

% ----- 2. choose the smallest colour among the candidates -----
special_color(S) :- maxColor(S), S = #min { X : maxColor(X) }.

% The solver is forced to use this colour; we add a global constraint:
:- not special_color(S).   % S must be the colour selected above.

% ----- 3. rows / columns that contain the special colour -----
row_has_S(R) :- special_color(S), input(R,_,S).
col_has_S(C) :- special_color(S), input(_,C,S).

output_cell(R,C) :- row_has_S(R), col_has_S(C).

% ----- 4. write the two possible colours for each cell -----
output(R,C,S)  :- output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,S).
output(R,C,0)  :- output_cell(R,C), not output(R,C,S).

% ----- 5. no output outside the rectangle -----
:- output(R,C,V), not output_cell(R,C), color(V).

% ----- 6. forbid any colour other than S or 0 inside the rectangle -----
:- output(R,C,V), V != S, V != 0, output_cell(R,C).

% ----- 7. uniqueness: exactly one colour per cell -----
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).

```

```asp
% -------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • Find the most frequent non‑zero colour in the input.
%   • Keep that colour (call it S) unchanged.
%   • The output grid is exactly the Cartesian product of rows and
%     columns that contain at least one S.
%   • Inside that rectangle cells are S where the input contains S,
%     otherwise 0.
%   • No other colour may appear.
%   • Exactly one colour must be assigned to each cell.
% -------------------------------------------------------------

% 1. predicate for colours that are not background (ignore 0)
nonZero(C) :- color(C), C != 0.

% 2. the maximal count among the non‑zero colours
maxCount = #max { C : nonZero(C), #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) } }.

% 3. colours that achieve the maximal count
maxColor(C) :- #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) } = maxCount, nonZero(C).

% 4. pick the smallest colour among the candidates
special_color(S) :- maxColor(S), S = #min { X : maxColor(X) }.

% 5. S must be the colour we have just selected
:- not special_color(S).

% 5. rows and columns that contain the chosen colour S
row_has_S(R) :- special_color(S), input(R,_,S).
col_has_S(C) :- special_color(S), input(_,C,S).

% 6. output domain = all pairs (row,col) where both belong to the
%    set of rows/columns that contain S
output_cell(R,C) :- row_has_S(R), col_has_S(C).

% 6. colour the cells: foreground where S is present, background otherwise
output(R,C,S)  :- output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,S).
output(R,C,0)  :- output_cell(R,C), not output(R,C,S).

% 7. forbid any colour outside the rectangle
:- output(R,C,V), not output_cell(R,C), color(V).

% 8. forbid colours other than S or 0 inside the rectangle
:- output(R,C,V), V != S, V != 0, output_cell(R,C).

% 9. exactly one colour per cell (uniqueness)
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).
```

candidates.response:
**Explanation**

The puzzles always keep one colour – the colour that appears most often (ignoring the background colour 0) – unchanged, turn every other colour into 0 and the output grid is exactly the rectangular region that contains that colour.  

*Example 1*: colour 3 is the most frequent non‑zero colour → the rectangle that contains a 3 is the 5 × 5 region shown in the output; cells inside the rectangle are 3 where the input has a 3 and 0 elsewhere.  
*Example 2*: colour 6 is the most frequent non‑zero colour → a 5 × 5 rectangle around the 6’s is produced, 6 where present, 0 elsewhere.  
*Example 3*: colour 5 is the foreground → the same rule yields the 5 × 5 output.

The encoding follows this deterministic rule:

1. **Pick the foreground colour**  
   *`nonZero(C)`* selects colours `≠ 0`.  
   *`maxCount`* is the largest count among the `nonZero` colours.  
   *`maxColor(C)`* are the colours whose count equals `maxCount`.  
   *`special_color(S)`* picks the smallest of them (breaks ties).  
   A global constraint forces `S` to be exactly that colour.

2. **Define the output domain**  
   `row_has_S(R)` is true for rows that contain at least one `S`.  
   `col_has_S(C)` is true for columns that contain at least one `S`.  
   `output_cell(R,C)` is the Cartesian product of those rows and columns – the exact rectangle the puzzle expects.

3. **Write the two possible colours for each cell**  
   *`output(R,C,S)`* is forced when the input already contains `S`.  
   *`output(R,C,0)`* is forced for every other cell of the rectangle.

4. **Guarantee correctness**  
   * forbid any output outside the rectangle;  
   * forbid any colour other than `S` or 0 inside the rectangle;  
   * enforce uniqueness (exactly one colour per cell).

The program below implements the whole reasoning with only regular rules and aggregates – no choice rules are used, therefore Clingo will return a single, uniquely defined answer set that matches the expected output.

```asp
% -------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • Find the most frequent non‑zero colour in the input.
%   • Keep this colour (call it S) unchanged.
%   • The output grid is exactly the Cartesian product of
%     rows and columns that contain at least one S.
%   • Inside this rectangle cells are S where the input contains S,
%     otherwise 0.
%   • No other colour may appear.
%   • Exactly one colour must be assigned to each cell.
% -------------------------------------------------------------

% 1. predicate for colours that are not background (ignore 0)
nonZero(C) :- color(C), C != 0.

% 2. maximal count among the non‑zero colours
maxCount = #max { C : nonZero(C), #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) } }.

% 3. colours that achieve the maximal count
maxColor(C) :- #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) } = maxCount, nonZero(C).

% 4. pick the smallest colour among the candidates
special_color(S) :- maxColor(S), S = #min { X : maxColor(X) }.

% 5. the chosen colour must be used – forces the solver to pick it
:- not special_color(S).

% 5. rows and columns that contain the chosen colour S
row_has_S(R) :- special_color(S), input(R,_,S).
col_has_S(C) :- special_color(S), input(_,C,S).

% 6. output domain = all pairs (row,col) where both belong to the
%    set of rows/columns that contain S
output_cell(R,C) :- row_has_S(R), col_has_S(C).

% 6. colour the cells: foreground where S is present, background otherwise
output(R,C,S) :- output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,S).
output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not output(R,C,S).

% 7. forbid any colour outside the rectangle
:- output(R,C,V), not output_cell(R,C), color(V).

% 8. forbid any colour other than S or 0 inside the rectangle
:- output(R,C,V), V != S, V != 0, output_cell(R,C).

% 9. exactly one colour per cell (uniqueness)
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).
```

candidates.program_final:
% -------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • Find the most frequent non‑zero colour in the input.
%   • Keep this colour (call it S) unchanged.
%   • The output grid is exactly the Cartesian product of
%     rows and columns that contain at least one S.
%   • Inside this rectangle cells are S where the input contains S,
%     otherwise 0.
%   • No other colour may appear.
%   • Exactly one colour must be assigned to each cell.
% -------------------------------------------------------------

% 1. colours that are not background (ignore 0)
nonZero(C) :- color(C), C != 0.

% 2. count of each colour
count_color(C,N) :- nonZero(C), N = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% 3. maximal count among the colour counts
maxCount(MC) :- MC = #max { N : count_color(_,N) }.

% 4. colours that achieve the maximal count
maxColor(C) :- count_color(C,N), N = MC, nonZero(C).

% 5. pick the smallest colour among the candidates
special_color(S) :- maxColor(S), S = #min { X : maxColor(X) }.

% 6. rows and columns that contain the chosen colour S
row_has_S(R) :- special_color(S), input(R,_,S).
col_has_S(C) :- special_color(S), input(_,C,S).

% 7. output domain = all pairs (row,col) where both belong to the
%    set of rows/columns that contain S
output_cell(R,C) :- row_has_S(R), col_has_S(C).

% 8. colour the cells: S inside, 0 outside
output(R,C,S) :- output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,S).
output(R,C,0) :- special_color(S), output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,S).

% 9. forbid any colour outside the rectangle
:- output(R,C,V), not output_cell(R,C), color(V).

% 10. forbid colours other than S or 0 inside the rectangle
:- special_color(S), output(R,C,V), V != S, V != 0, output_cell(R,C).

% 11. exactly one colour per cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).

% 12. choice of colour for each output cell (exactly one)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% -------------------------------------------------------------

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% -------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • Find the most frequent non‑zero colour in the input.
%   • Keep this colour (call it S) unchanged.
%   • The output grid is exactly the Cartesian product of
%     rows and columns that contain at least one S.
%   • Inside this rectangle cells are S where the input contains S,
%     otherwise 0.
%   • No other colour may appear.
%   • Exactly one colour must be assigned to each cell.
% -------------------------------------------------------------

% 1. colours that are not background (ignore 0)
nonZero(C) :- color(C), C != 0.

% 2. count of each colour
count_color(C,N) :- nonZero(C), N = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% 3. maximal count
maxCount(MC) :- MC = #max { N : count_color(_,N) }.

% 4. colours that achieve maximal count
maxColor(C) :- count_color(C,N), N = MC, nonZero(C).

% 5. pick the smallest colour among the candidates
special_color(S) :- maxColor(S), S = #min { X : maxColor(X) }.

% 6. rows and columns that contain the chosen colour S
row_has_S(R) :- special_color(S), input(R,_,S).
col_has_S(C) :- special_color(S), input(_,C,S).

% 7. output domain = all pairs (row,col) where both belong to the
%    set of rows/columns that contain S
output_cell(R,C) :- row_has_S(R), col_has_S(C).

% 8. colour the cells: S inside, 0 outside
output(R,C,S) :- output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,S).
output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,S).

% 9. forbid any colour outside the rectangle
:- output(R,C,V), not output_cell(R,C), color(V).

% 10. forbid colours other than S or 0 inside the rectangle
:- output(R,C,V), V != S, V != 0, output_cell(R,C).

% 11. exactly one colour per cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).
```

% -------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • Find the most frequent non‑zero colour in the input.
%   • Keep this colour (call it S) unchanged.
%   • The output grid is exactly the Cartesian product of
%     rows and columns that contain at least one S.
%   • Inside this rectangle cells are S where the input contains S,
%     otherwise 0.
%   • No other colour may appear.
%   • Exactly one colour must be assigned to each cell.
% -------------------------------------------------------------

% 1. predicate for colours that are not background (ignore 0)
nonZero(C) :- color(C), C != 0.

% 2. maximal count among the non‑zero colours
maxCount = #max { C : nonZero(C), #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) } }.

% 3. colours that achieve the maximal count
maxColor(C) :- #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) } = maxCount, nonZero(C).

% 4. pick the smallest colour among the candidates
special_color(S) :- maxColor(S), S = #min { X : maxColor(X) }.

% 5. the chosen colour must be used – forces the solver to pick it
:- not special_color(S).

% 5. rows and columns that contain the chosen colour S
row_has_S(R) :- special_color(S), input(R,_,S).
col_has_S(C) :- special_color(S), input(_,C,S).

output_cell(R,C) :- row_has_S(R), col_has_S(C).

% 6. colour the cells: foreground where S is present, background otherwise
output(R,C,S) :- output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,S).
output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not output(R,C,S).

% 7. forbid any colour outside the rectangle
:- output(R,C,V), not output_cell(R,C), color(V).

% 8. forbid any colour other than S or 0 inside the rectangle

% 9. exactly one colour per cell (uniqueness)
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).
